claimed_contribution: The paper develops and validates a new scenario-based Response-to-Failure (RTF) scale to predict behavior after an initial self-control lapse.
It empirically distinguishes initial self-control failure from post-failure behavior and shows they are not captured by the same predictors.
It shows that the same cognitive and emotional response structure applies across eating, spending, and cheating, and provides short-form domain scales.
context: The paper studies consumer sequential self-control decisions after an initial lapse. The focal domains are eating, spending, and cheating, using mostly undergraduate samples in scenario, lab-task, diary, and survey settings.
design_and_data: The authors ran prestudies to generate and screen items from open-ended responses to self-control-failure scenarios (Prestudy A, N=75; Prestudy B, N=194), then used exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses to select a two-factor, 63-item scale. Psychometric validation included test-retest and social desirability checks (Study 1, N=48), nomological and discriminant validity against multiple related scales (Study 2, N=124), and extension to a cheating domain scale (Study 3, N=43). Predictive validity was tested in scenario experiments with manipulated initial failure in spending (Study 4, N=167) and eating (Study 5, N=144), a one-week diary of eating behavior (Study 6, N=148), a lab cheating paradigm with incentive manipulation (Study 7, N=115), and a debt outcome study (Study 8, N=120). They also reanalyzed results to develop 18-item short forms for each domain.
main_findings: Across eating, spending, and cheating, the RTF items consistently formed two factors: a tendency toward additional failure and a tendency toward reform, with evidence of reliability and nomological/discriminant validity.
RTF scores predicted post-failure behavior when an initial failure had occurred, but did not predict behavior in no-initial-failure conditions or the occurrence of initial failure itself.
RTF predictions held beyond existing self-control-related scales and extended to behavioral outcomes such as escalating cheating and higher reported credit card debt.
one_line_summary: The paper develops and validates the Response-to-Failure scale, a scenario-based measure of cognitive and emotional reactions to an initial lapse that predicts whether consumers reform or fail again across eating, spending, and cheating.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior self-control research by moving from single-choice models to sequential post-failure behavior and by jointly examining initial failure, integral emotions and cognitions, and subsequent action. It introduces a measure that predicts post-failure behavior beyond existing trait and domain self-control scales.
research_gap: Most existing self-control research and scales focus on single choices and do not directly predict behavior after an initial lapse. Prior work had also tended to examine failure effects on emotions/cognitions and emotions/cognitions effects on behavior separately, rather than jointly for post-failure behavior.
research_question: Which cognitive and emotional responses to an initial self-control failure determine whether consumers show additional failure or reform? Can a scenario-based Response-to-Failure scale capture these responses and predict post-failure behavior across eating, spending, and cheating beyond existing self-control measures?
